author: @ToninFightsEntropy
text: That's exactly what I do! (Minus having to bypass a sensor). Best way for sure! Though if you open the feeder and push too hard it can make a wider bit on a layer, but it's nothing a scalpel can't scrape off.

author: @canadamadman5306
text: Why not just wait for the printer to ask for it? It will pause and wait.? Seems like unnecessary risk to the print

author: @SchwaAlien
text: This is even easier (but potentially less successful) on the Bowden style printers because the extruder stepper is stationary but then the potential problems come from the fact that it cannot fully retract once the new filament passes the gear since it’s not attached to the old filament until the new filament reaches the hot end but it hasn’t caused me any real issues despite doing a few multi-day 1kg+ prints and lots of using up ends of rolls on smaller prints.

author: @jcenergy2493
text:
This is a recipe for disaster if the quality of the print is important. The end can get hung inside and won't finish pushing through. 

Waiting for an interior portion to be printing when near the end of a roll, pause then change. Restart. This guarantees a fast easy change that will NEVER fail or effect your quality.

author: @logothaironsides2942
text: Clever. My printer is a prusa clone I put together from a kit  and a few years old now.  I think myself lucky if I get a print after 3 tries and I would be flabbergasted if it allowed me to do what your printer will allow.  Mine does have the sensor but I think I don't use the machine enough to get really adept at picking up skills for coping with the snags that I encounter. It has enabled me to mend various gadgets that would otherwise have needed costly replacement or just binning so its all good.

author: @DaremoKamen
text: Off topic of this video but I've had an idea about some of the small scale wind turbines.  If someone didn't want to combine the small scale turbines for larger power and redundancy,  what if you combined one with one of those inexpensive solar LED lights?  You would already have a load and an energy storage system.  If you just wanted a light, the turbine would help keep the battery charged even at night.  Or you could just use them as chargers for AA or AAA size batteries.

author: @jamest.5001
text:
Are you going to try making your own filament?  I have seen people use grocery bags, and water and drink bottles, to make filament , it doesn't seem the bags would be a good idea, I know it's thin, but the ones we get, if the sun shines on it for a day, it turns to dust, and you are lucky to get your goods home in them, I don't know much about 3D printing, but if I did get one, I would be learning fast,   and the carbon fiber filament, I'd like to check that stuff out!!     I have mind full of things I'd like to make,   maybe one day I can build a shop with a printer ,and use that printer to make a large printer,   I'd like to make large things and a bed with a sq. Meter, and nearly 2meters tall, (2.5meters tall probably) would be great, and a starting point, be capable of using a whole roll of filament on a single print,  maybe have multiple print heads, and spool holders. I have been studying printers , how they are built, and the controls,   and the hu'normas ones that print with concrete,  that's awesome!  If there was a better way  to add rebar, a couple guys could build a huge house in a couple days!   And limited by imagination only.. 
 Plus  using 3D prints for casting  molds, making cast steel and aluminum parts would be great, when I first heard of 3D printing, I thought that would be the primary use, was I wrong or what,  I do not like the idea of printing parts in sections, gluing them together, one thing I'd really like to make , is a huge sterling engine, something nearly resembling a v4 engine, displacing nearly 30 litres per "cylinder" that is to be heated, use possibly an oil to to be heated by concentrated sun, using multiple sat dishes, tracking the sun,  and using water pumped  through a water fall and a small pond to keep the cool side cool!  And if nothing else, spin it with a motor, powered by solar power, to make a heat pump to cool my off grid home, I could not believe seeing a sterling engine spun, and it making ice from the water condensed on the case,  that was mind blowing! It is not efficient , but if you have such a large sterling, and solar to spin it, and use it to cool water with ethanol, or antifreeze in it, cooled to about -10° +/-,  circulated through radiators that are also used for heating in winter with a wood stove/ boiler, and heat exchanger, it can be useful, hopefully dropping the temperature 20°F in a 1800 sq ft home, keeping some parts closed off,  a 3D printer could build that!  It's mind blowing,   sorry to ramble again! If you got this far, Happy New year!! 🎉. Thanks for everything,

author: @Enjoymentboy
text: I had a buddy who worked in a plant that made steel cables.  They used a cold welding machine to join spools of wire.  The end of the old wire in one end and the end of the new wire in the other.  Clamp the ends down and push down a lever.   Pull the lever up and both ends are now joined with a seamless weld.  It was quite impressive.  Surprised they don't have something like this for filaments.

replies.author: @Enjoymentboy
replies.text: @UnitSe7en Neither are 3d printers but there's lots of them in use.  Lol.  It just seems to me there's a better way to do this than to sit there and wait for the spool to run out.  If you were able to weld/join the end of one spool to the beginning of another and just walk away that would simplify things and remove some of the risk of a failed print.  But what works for one person won't work for another.  Personally I'm not sitting by a machine waiting for it to run out of materials if there is a way to pre-stock it so that I can do other stuff while the machine is working.

replies.author: @newmonengineering
replies.text: They have a hand held splicing device that can do this. I have never tried it but some people use it. It would be nice to have it built onto the top of a printer though where you could simply splice the next spool it as it feeds through but the current cost is about 50 of 60$ for one.

author: @dav1dbone
text: Making the blades solid seems wasteful Rob, you could have made them hollow or reduced the infill with honeycomb/hex shapes, if you did want a more robust or heavier result a hollow print could be done and then filled with paper mache etc afterwards.

author: @kreynolds1123
text: Wondering how difficult it would be to thermally weld the tail end to the beginning of the new without creating ledges that may impede automatic feeding.

replies.author: @jakobhansen1396
replies.text: Not especially, but a couple of problems impede the process, one is thermal expansion and contraction, another is loss of strengt in the joint. Both can be mitigated by sliding a bit of PTFE tube on one string, then melt and weld the two ends, sliding the PTFE tube over the joint and shaping the molten plastic. Leave the tube on the joint for protection.

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text:
@jakobhansen1396given sane materials, expansion and contraction between filaments are likely to be identical. But being mostly lengthwise the joint shouldn't be under stress.

Maybe there is an opportunity here. I can see using a peltier to warm and cool two halfs of a die for welding two filaments of given diameter.

replies.author: @jakobhansen1396
replies.text:
@kreynolds1123 in practice there is a certain swelling that needs to be managed, filament is usually extruded with a specifik pull forming the diameter, which is hard to replicate. 
Regarding strengt it's the mostly part that is important, the coils introduce bending and imperfections in the weld become significant.

author: @peterrockell1527
text: If you don't use the filament runout sensor, just stick a short length of filament through it, rather than feeding the whole reel through it and then keep an eye out for when you need to poke the new filament into the feeder :-)

author: @MakerBees333
text:
On these larger shapes with small or no retractions you can get away with this. When small articulated items with many retractions need more filament it would probably be easiest (assuming their is no pause button in the UI) is trip the filament sensor.  

It always works until it doesn’t, and one big fail is all it takes to cancel  out all that time and effort you made to use every drop of filament. Best of luck, there is no correct answer but as my experience has grown I have stopped doing the demonstrated method all together.

replies.author: @pturcanu
replies.text:
Has worked for me on a bowden-tube ender 3, even with retractions. I just continue pushing new filament into extruder despite retractions, until it is some 10cm into the tube. True, there might be more stringing while the remainder of the old filament is used up, but hasn't yet made any prints unusable.
Plus, one would have to be watching the print and be close by to change the filament, and without a filament sensor a timing mistake might be costly...

author: @Spinningininfinity
text: There's no spool like an old spool ❤️

replies.author: @baldeagle5297
replies.text: I see what you did there.

author: @FreeEnergyNerd
text:
How long does one spool last? that depends on the quality of your print (if you print in 0.12 or 0.28mm) ... that means you have to check your print all the time because you have to be there BEFORE your sensor gives an alert.  I let the sensor give me an alert (very loud siren - it will then continue printing for another 5 to 10 minutes depending on the print). It would even pull me out of bed in the middle of the night even when i'm sleeping. I then go and press the "pause" button, which makes the head go in a corner. I then change the filament (letting 10 cm come through the nozzle) . when that is done i hit the "resume button" and it continues where it paused. depending on the sensor, it opens or closes a circuit. those filament sensors are less than 10$. you can easily connect an extra one and almost any alarm, and its much better than having to wait and watch.. I'm not saying your method is bad, just that i wouldn't have the time waiting till the spool is finished. 
Luc

author: @grendel1960a
text: luckily on my printer the feed gear is remote at the end of a tube to the print head, but essentially its the same method, just feed the new filament into the top end of the tube, the butt joint between the two allowing it to continue pushing through to the print head.
